explanation_en: 'Weg' means 'way', 'path', or 'route'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.de: _____ Weg durch den Wald ist schön.
question.en: The path through the forest is beautiful.

explanation_en: 'Weg' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ zur Schule dauert zehn Minuten.
question.en: The way to school takes ten minutes.

explanation_en: 'Weg' means 'way' or 'path'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: I'm afraid I don't know the way.

explanation_en: 'kennen' takes the accusative. 'den Weg' — masculine accusative uses 'den'. The noun is unchanged.
'Platz' means 'place', 'seat', or 'square'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.de: _____ Platz vor dem Rathaus ist sehr groß.
question.en: The square in front of the town hall is very large.

explanation_en: 'Platz' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ neben mir ist noch frei.
question.en: The seat next to me is still free.

explanation_en: 'Platz' means 'seat' or 'place'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: May I take this seat?

explanation_en: 'nehmen' takes the accusative. 'diesen Platz' — masculine accusative uses 'diesen' before the unchanged noun.
'Seite' means 'side' or 'page'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The page of the book is damaged.

explanation_en: 'Seite' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: Page three contains the answer.

explanation_en: 'Seite' means 'page'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: Bitte lest die nächste _____ im Buch.
question.en: Please read the next page in the book.

explanation_en: 'lesen' takes the accusative. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'die nächste Seite'.
'Art' means 'type', 'kind', or 'way of doing something'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.de: _____ Art, wie er spricht, gefällt mir.
question.en: The way he speaks appeals to me.

explanation_en: 'Art' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Diese _____ zu lernen ist sehr effektiv.
question.en: This way of learning is very effective.

explanation_en: 'Art' means 'way' or 'kind'. After 'diese' (feminine nominative), the noun is unchanged.

question.de: Es gibt viele verschiedene _____ von Tieren.
question.en: There are many different kinds of animals.

explanation_en: The plural of 'Art' is 'Arten'. 'viele verschiedene Arten' uses the plural accusative form of the noun.
'Name' means 'name'. It is a masculine noun — and a weak noun (N-Deklination), taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The name of the restaurant is unknown.

explanation_en: 'Name' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ der Stadt ist sehr bekannt.
question.en: The name of the city is very well known.

explanation_en: 'Name' means 'name'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: I have forgotten his name.

explanation_en: 'Name' is a weak masculine noun. In the accusative, it adds -n: 'seinen Namen'. This is the N-Deklination pattern.
'Kopf' means 'head'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: My head hurts.

explanation_en: 'Kopf' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ ist der oberste Teil des Körpers.
question.en: The head is the uppermost part of the body.

explanation_en: 'Kopf' means 'head'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Er schüttelt den _____ und sagt nichts.
question.en: He shakes his head and says nothing.

explanation_en: 'schütteln' takes the accusative. 'den Kopf' — masculine accusative uses 'den'. The noun is unchanged.
'Hand' means 'hand'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The hand is very cold.

explanation_en: 'Hand' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ des Kindes ist sehr klein.
question.en: The child's hand is very small.

explanation_en: 'Hand' means 'hand'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: Er gibt mir die _____ zur Begrüßung.
question.en: He gives me his hand in greeting.

explanation_en: 'geben' takes a dative object (mir) and accusative object (die Hand). Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'die Hand'.
'Herz' means 'heart'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The heart is beating very fast.

explanation_en: 'Herz' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The heart is an important organ.

explanation_en: 'Herz' means 'heart'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Er hat ein großes _____ und hilft immer anderen.
question.en: He has a big heart and always helps others.

explanation_en: After 'ein großes' (neuter accusative adjective), 'Herz' is unchanged — neuter nominative and accusative are identical.
'Stimme' means 'voice'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The voice is very loud.

explanation_en: 'Stimme' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The singer's voice is wonderful.

explanation_en: 'Stimme' means 'voice'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.en: I recognise his voice immediately.

explanation_en: 'erkennen' takes the accusative. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'seine Stimme'.
'Gesicht' means 'face'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.de: _____ Gesicht des Kindes ist sehr süß.
question.en: The child's face is very sweet.

explanation_en: 'Gesicht' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The face shows what he feels.

explanation_en: 'Gesicht' means 'face'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Sie wäscht ihr _____ mit kaltem Wasser.
question.en: She washes her face with cold water.

explanation_en: After 'ihr' (possessive, neuter accusative), 'Gesicht' is unchanged — neuter nominative and accusative are identical.
'Zug' means 'train'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The train departs at 9 o'clock.

explanation_en: 'Zug' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ nach Berlin fährt von Gleis drei.
question.en: The train to Berlin leaves from platform three.

explanation_en: 'Zug' means 'train'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich nehme den nächsten _____ nach Hamburg.
question.en: I am taking the next train to Hamburg.

explanation_en: 'nehmen' takes the accusative. 'den nächsten Zug' — masculine accusative uses 'den'.
'Preis' means both 'price' and 'prize'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The price is very high.

explanation_en: 'Preis' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ für das Ticket ist gestiegen.
question.en: The price for the ticket has gone up.

explanation_en: 'Preis' means 'price'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Sie hat den ersten _____ im Wettbewerb gewonnen.
question.en: She won first prize in the competition.

explanation_en: 'gewinnen' takes the accusative. 'den ersten Preis' — masculine accusative uses 'den'.
